rationale:
Grounded in source, both imports are functionally identical for <Workflow>, and the code is intentional — this is a docs clarity gap, not a library defect.

Code facts:
- packages/smithers/src/create.js:238-240 — the factory `Workflow` is a pure pass-through: `function Workflow(props) { return React.createElement(BaseWorkflow, props, props.children); }`. It adds NO props and NO context provider.
- create.js:13 imports `Workflow as BaseWorkflow` from "@smithers-orchestrator/components", which is the exact same component exported top-level from "smithers-orchestrator" (packages/smithers/src/index.js:180 re-exports `Workflow` from @smithers-orchestrator/components; packages/components/src/components/index.js:83 → Workflow.js, which just emits `<smithers:workflow>`). So `createSmithers().Workflow` and `import { Workflow } from "smithers-orchestrator"` resolve to the identical render output.
- Contrast with the components that DO need the factory: create.js Task/Approval/Sandbox/Signal inject `smithersContext: RuntimeSmithersContext`. But even the top-level `Task` works under a factory tree because `boundSmithers` wraps the build in BOTH RuntimeSmithersContext.Provider AND GlobalSmithersContext.Provider (create.js:~308) and top-level Task defaults `smithersContext` to the global SmithersContext (packages/components/src/components/Task.js:208). This is why pi-hello-world.jsx can import Workflow AND Task top-level while only destructuring { smithers, outputs } from the factory and still run correctly.

Docs/examples facts:
- docs/components/workflow.mdx (the authoritative <Workflow> reference) shows BOTH forms without reconciling them: line 7 `import { Workflow } from "smithers-orchestrator"` in the type block, then line 22 `const { Workflow, Task, smithers, outputs } = createSmithers({...})` in the runnable example.
- examples/: 95 files destructure Workflow from createSmithers; only 2 (pi-hello-world.jsx, pi-tools-workflow.jsx) import it top-level — and those still mix (top-level Workflow+Task, factory smithers+outputs).
- skills/smithers/SKILL.md:120 imports `Sequence, Task` top-level but destructures `Workflow` from createSmithers, modeling the mixed style without explaining it.

Conclusion: real candidate friction, correct intentional behavior. Fix = state in the <Workflow> reference doc that both imports are equivalent and the destructure form is the convention. Not a library issue (no bug; the equivalence is by design and removing either path would break valid existing examples).</rationale>
<parameter name="docFix">{"file":"docs/components/workflow.mdx","anchorLine":"- Direct children run sequentially; `<Sequence>` is only needed inside other control-flow components.","markdown":"- **Two ways to get `<Workflow>`, both equivalent.** You can either `import { Workflow } from \"smithers-orchestrator\"` (top-level export) or destructure it from `createSmithers(...)` — they render the identical node. The factory's `Workflow` is a thin pass-through to the same component (it injects no schema or context), unlike `Task`/`Approval`/`Sandbox`/`Signal`, which the factory wires to the run's context and your output schemas. **Convention: destructure `Workflow` from `createSmithers` alongside `Task`, `smithers`, and `outputs`** so the workflow's components all come from one place; this is what the example above and ~95% of `examples/` do. Importing `Workflow` (or `Sequence`, `Branch`, `Loop`, `Parallel`, which are also pure structural components) directly from `\"smithers-orchestrator\"` is equally valid and is handy in tiny single-file workflows."}

evalRef: authoring-workflows (candidate friction); grounded by knowledge-concepts cases knowledge-structured-output--{haiku,sonnet,gemini} and authoring-workflows cases sdk-agent-structured-output--{haiku,kimi} / codex-schema-output--{gemini,kimi}
rationale: Verified in source, not guessed. AnthropicAgent sets `supportsNativeStructuredOutput = true` (packages/agents/src/AnthropicAgent.js:20) and uses the AI SDK `Output.object({ schema })` native path (AnthropicAgent.js:39-41). OpenAIAgent sets it from `nativeStructuredOutput` defaulting to true (OpenAIAgent.js:43,53). ClaudeCodeAgent extends BaseCliAgent (ClaudeCodeAgent.js:89) and BaseCliAgent never sets the flag, so it is undefined/falsy. The engine reads `effectiveAgent.supportsNativeStructuredOutput === true` (packages/engine/src/engine.js:3230) and, when a task has an output schema but the flag is false, it console.warns "engine ... does not support native structured output. Falling back to prompt-injection + text JSON extraction ... consider switching to an engine that declares supportsNativeStructuredOutput=true (Anthropic, OpenAI)" (engine.js:3235-3239) and injects JSON instructions into the prompt (engine.js:3240-3249). Nuance confirmed: CodexAgent does write a JSON-Schema temp file and pass `--output-schema` to the CLI (CodexAgent.js:554-563) for CLI-level constrained decoding, but it still does NOT set supportsNativeStructuredOutput, so the engine wraps it with the same prompt-injection fallback; ClaudeCodeAgent only honors a manual `jsonSchema` constructor flag (mapped to `--json-schema`, ClaudeCodeAgent.js:408) and gets no auto-wiring from the task outputSchema. This matches the eval rubric ("AnthropicAgent/OpenAIAgent native. CLI agents (ClaudeCodeAgent/CodexAgent/etc) support via outputSchema parameter"). The library behavior is deliberate and already surfaces a runtime warning (and ctx.output() field-stripping per changelog 0.24.0), so this is not a code defect. The gap is discoverability: the agent-selection docs do not tell authors to prefer SDK agents for typed/JSON output. docs/integrations/sdk-agents.mdx has a "CLI vs SDK" table (lines 137-145) comparing Billing/Tools/Flexibility but omitting structured output; the only mentions of native-vs-prompt are buried in changelogs and the Hermes/local-server disable case. docs/components/task.mdx:170 even over-generalizes ("When outputSchema is set, JSON is extracted from agent text"), which is only true for CLI agents. The fix is a doc note in the CLI vs SDK comparison.

docFix.anchorLine: | Flexibility | Native CLI flags | AI SDK `providerOptions` |
docFix.markdown:
| Structured output | Prompt-injection + text JSON extraction | Native (`supportsNativeStructuredOutput`) |

**Choosing an agent for typed/JSON-output tasks.** Only `AnthropicAgent` and `OpenAIAgent` declare `supportsNativeStructuredOutput = true`, so when a `<Task>` has an `output` schema they pass it through the AI SDK's native structured-output API (`Output.object({ schema })`) and the result is schema-constrained. CLI agents (`ClaudeCodeAgent`, `CodexAgent`, etc.) do **not** set this flag: the engine falls back to injecting JSON instructions into the prompt and extracting the object from the model's text, and emits a `console.warn` ("engine ... does not support native structured output. Falling back to prompt-injection + text JSON extraction"). Schema-validation retries still run, but valid JSON shape does not guarantee meaningful values. For tasks whose primary job is producing typed/JSON output, prefer `AnthropicAgent`/`OpenAIAgent` (or, for an OpenAI-compatible endpoint that honors JSON schema, an `OpenAIAgent` with `nativeStructuredOutput` left enabled). `CodexAgent` additionally forwards the task schema to the CLI via `--output-schema` for constrained decoding, but the engine still wraps it with the prompt-injection fallback because the flag is unset.

rationale: Grounded in the real source: `packages/smithers/src/create.js` defines `boundSmithers(build, smithersOpts)` (line 294) whose `build: (ctx) => ...` is a plain JS function invoked as `build(ctx)` (line 307). Because it is an ordinary function parameter, the builder may ignore `ctx` entirely. The public type in `packages/smithers/src/CreateSmithersApi.ts:55-58` is `smithers(build: (ctx: SmithersCtx<...>) => React.ReactElement, opts?)`; TypeScript's standard parameter-arity assignability lets you pass a zero-arg `() => ...` where a one-arg builder is expected, so `ctx` is genuinely optional. This is confirmed by real docs that already drop it: `docs/components/supervisor.mdx:27`, `docs/components/review-loop.mdx:24`, `docs/components/signal.mdx:36`, `docs/components/runbook.mdx:31`, `docs/components/content-pipeline.mdx:26`, `docs/components/optimizer.mdx:25`, and `docs/integrations/cli-agents.mdx:70` all use `smithers(() => (...))`. So the LIBRARY is correct — no code change is warranted. The gap is purely documentation: the canonical authoring reference `docs/jsx/overview.mdx` only ever shows `smithers((ctx) => ...)` and, at line 68, explains what `ctx` exposes (`ctx.input`, `ctx.outputMaybe`), but never states that you can omit `ctx` when your workflow has no dynamic input/output references. A candidate reading the primary reference reasonably concludes `ctx` is mandatory boilerplate even though static workflows elsewhere in the same docs drop it — exactly the authoring-workflows candidate friction. A one-line clarification right after the sentence describing `ctx` resolves it.

docFix.anchorLine: The tree re-renders on every frame, so branching is a normal JSX conditional. Inside a workflow function, `ctx` exposes `ctx.input` and `ctx.outputMaybe(ref, { nodeId })`. The latter returns the output of a completed task, or `undefined` if it hasn't run yet:
docFix.markdown: The `ctx` parameter is optional: it is just a normal function argument, so a workflow that never reads `ctx.input` or `ctx.outputMaybe` can drop it entirely and write `smithers(() => ( ... ))`. Add `(ctx)` only when you actually reference dynamic input or prior task output.

rationale:
The imperative API the eval expects already exists AND is documented, so this is a docs-clarity gap, not a missing library API. Evidence:

- `packages/memory/src/store/MemoryStore.ts:8-29` defines `getFact(ns,key)`, `setFact(ns,key,value,ttlMs?)`, `deleteFact(ns,key)`, `listFacts(ns)` (plus `*Effect` variants), and `MemoryServiceApi` (`packages/memory/src/MemoryServiceApi.ts:9-49`) exposes the same plus `readonly store: MemoryStore`.
- These are documented in `docs/concepts/memory.mdx:32-36` (and the llms-memory.txt fragment lines 37-41). The eval reference answers (cases.jsonl) literally are `store.setFact({kind:'workflow',id:'my-flow'},'model','gpt-4')`, `store.getFact(...)`, `store.deleteFact(...)` — i.e., the exact documented surface. So candidates that "guessed store.setFact()" were guessing the CORRECT call.

The real gap is the in-workflow access pattern. The eval report (`evals/harness/.report/authoring-memory-haiku.json`) shows the candidate PASSED set-fact-basic but with oneShot=false and two explicit friction items it self-reported: (1) ambiguous-docs: "Memory store access within a Smithers workflow context is not explicitly documented; had to infer ... that creating a store within a Task's async body is valid"; (2) missing-docs: "No explicit example showing how to imperatively call store.setFact() from within a running Smithers workflow (only Task metadata declarative memory with recall/remember is shown)."

Confirmed from source: a compute `<Task>` callback receives only `deps` (`docs/components/task.mdx:64-66`); there is NO injected store/memory handle. So the only way to imperatively get/set/delete a fact during a run is to build a store with `createMemoryStore(db)` inside a compute Task body and call `await store.setFact(...)`. The docs show store creation only at top-level-script altitude and present `memory={{recall,remember}}` as the in-Task path, while `memory.mdx:63` even calls that block "metadata for runtimes and integrations" — which makes the declarative block sound like the imperative path when it is actually inert metadata. That missing bridge is what tanks one-shot confidence on get/set/delete-fact cases.

Fix: add a concrete in-workflow example to `docs/concepts/memory.mdx` showing the store created and used imperatively inside a compute `<Task>` body, and explicitly contrast it with the declarative `memory={{recall,remember}}` metadata. No library change needed.

docFix.anchorLine: `memory` is preserved on the task descriptor as metadata for runtimes and integrations that layer memory behavior onto task execution. The direct store APIs above are the current public memory read/write surface.

rationale: Confirmed root cause in the real scheduler source. Run terminal status is binary (finished | failed), computed in packages/scheduler/src/makeWorkflowSession.js. A run is marked failed only when unhandledFailureDecision() finds a failed task (defined at makeWorkflowSession.js:433). That function deliberately EXCLUDES two large classes of failed children: (1) any failed task whose descriptor.continueOnFail is set — makeWorkflowSession.js:438 `if (taskState === "failed" && !descriptor?.continueOnFail)`; and (2) failed AGENT tasks whose error is "transient" — makeWorkflowSession.js:442-444 `if (descriptor?.agent && isTransientSessionFailure(state.failures.get(key))) continue;`, where isTransientSessionFailure (makeWorkflowSession.js:186-196) returns true for SESSION_ERROR, TASK_TIMEOUT, TASK_HEARTBEAT_TIMEOUT, TASK_ABORTED, and failureRetryable. The eval's scenario is exactly this: a fan-out of agents that fail on rate limits (SESSION_ERROR-class) is skipped by the unhandled-failure check, so decide() falls through to finishedResult() (makeWorkflowSession.js:676) and returns status "finished" (surfaced to users as "completed"). finishedResult() (makeWorkflowSession.js:289-298) carries only runId/status/output — no failed-child count. The scheduler's parallel terminal computation reinforces this: scheduleTasks.js:158-168 marks a `parallel` node failed only when EVERY child failed (`failed: terminal && failed`), and isTerminal (scheduleTasks.js:16-22) treats a continueOnFail child's "failed" state as terminal-success, so a mostly-failed fan-out is never "failed". There is no degraded/partial status in RunResult.ts (status union: running|finished|failed|cancelled|continued|waiting-*) or RunStatusSchema.ts. DOCS side: docs/runtime/run-state.mdx — the canonical "what is this run doing" doc — maps `finished -> succeeded` (line 41) with zero caveat that a succeeded/finished run can mask failed child agents, and docs/components/parallel.mdx:35 promotes continueOnFail ("To let the group proceed past a failing child...") without warning it lets the run report success while children failed. So the agent-facing knowledge (eval rubric: "inspect per-node/agent outcomes... acknowledge that completed can mask failed children") is not documented anywhere. Both a doc clarification and a library status improvement are warranted.

docFix.anchorLine: `recovering` is reserved for the supervisor takeover window. As of this writing, it is defined but not yet emitted by the runtime.
docFix.markdown:
<Warning>
**`succeeded`/`finished` can mask failed child agents.** Run-level status is binary — `finished` or `failed` — and a run is only `failed` when it has an *unhandled* task failure. Two large classes of failed children are deliberately not treated as run-level failures: tasks marked [`continueOnFail`](/components/task), and agent tasks that fail with a *transient* error (rate limits, timeouts, aborts — `SESSION_ERROR`, `TASK_TIMEOUT`, `TASK_HEARTBEAT_TIMEOUT`, `TASK_ABORTED`, or `failureRetryable`). A fan-out where most agents hit provider rate limits can therefore still report `finished` → `succeeded`.

Never trust the top-level status alone to conclude a run truly succeeded. Inspect per-node/agent outcomes:

```bash
smithers inspect RUN_ID   # top-level runState plus every node's state (look for failed nodes)
smithers node NODE_ID      # one node's failure/error, retries, and tool calls
smithers events --run RUN_ID  # the full event history, including per-task failures
smithers scores RUN_ID     # scorer results when you gate on judged output
```

To avoid the rate-limit failures in the first place, bound fan-out concurrency with [`<Parallel maxConcurrency={N}>`](/components/parallel) so you don't burst past the provider's limit.
</Warning>

libraryIssue.title: Run reports `finished`/`succeeded` while most fan-out agents failed — add a degraded/partial-failure status (or surface failed-child counts)
libraryIssue.body:
### Eval
Surfaced by **real-usage `ru-run-completed-but-failed`** (`evals/suites/real-usage/cases.jsonl` lines 15-17). The eval task: *"My run reports status `completed` but most of its fan-out agents actually failed. How do I check whether the run truly succeeded?"* The rubric requires acknowledging that a `completed` status can mask failed children. Today the runtime makes this masking *unavoidable* for two common failure classes, with no first-class signal that anything went wrong.

### Root cause (real source)
Run terminal status is binary (`finished` | `failed`) and is decided in `packages/scheduler/src/makeWorkflowSession.js`:

- A run is only `failed` when `unhandledFailureDecision()` finds a failed task — `makeWorkflowSession.js:433`.
- That function **skips `continueOnFail` failures**: `makeWorkflowSession.js:438` → `if (taskState === "failed" && !descriptor?.continueOnFail)`.
- It **also skips transient agent failures**: `makeWorkflowSession.js:442-444` → `if (descriptor?.agent && isTransientSessionFailure(state.failures.get(key))) continue;`. `isTransientSessionFailure` (`makeWorkflowSession.js:186-196`) matches `SESSION_ERROR`, `TASK_TIMEOUT`, `TASK_HEARTBEAT_TIMEOUT`, `TASK_ABORTED`, and `failureRetryable === true` — i.e. exactly the rate-limit/timeout failures a large fan-out produces.
- With those skipped, `decide()` falls through to `finishedResult()` at `makeWorkflowSession.js:676`, returning `status: "finished"`.
- `finishedResult()` (`makeWorkflowSession.js:289-298`) carries only `{ runId, status, output }` — **no failed-child count**, so callers have nothing to gate on.

The scheduler reinforces this: in `packages/scheduler/src/scheduleTasks.js`, a `parallel` node is reported failed only when *every* child failed (`scheduleTasks.js:158-168`, `failed: terminal && failed`), and `isTerminal` (`scheduleTasks.js:16-22`) treats a `continueOnFail` child's `failed` state as terminal-success. So a fan-out where 9 of 10 agents fail is never `failed`.

The status unions have no room to express this: `packages/scheduler/src/RunResult.ts` (`running|finished|failed|cancelled|continued|waiting-*`) and `packages/engine/src/effect/RunStatusSchema.ts`. And `docs/runtime/run-state.mdx:41` maps `finished -> succeeded` with no caveat.

### Impact
Users and downstream automation read `status: finished` (→ `succeeded`) and conclude the run worked, when most of the work failed. There is no programmatic signal (status, count, or event) that the run finished in a degraded state; you must manually `smithers inspect` and eyeball per-node states.

### Suggested solution
1. **Track a degraded outcome.** In `decide()` / `finishedResult()` (`makeWorkflowSession.js:289`, `:676`), when the run reaches `finished` but `state.failures` contains tasks that were excluded by `unhandledFailureDecision` (the `continueOnFail` and transient-agent skips at `:438` and `:442`), record those keys. Surface them on the result, e.g. extend `finishedResult` output with `{ failedChildren: number, failedChildKeys: string[] }`.
2. **Add a degraded terminal status.** Introduce `"degraded"` (or `"partial"`) to `RunResult.ts` and `RunStatusSchema.ts`, returned by `finishedResult()` when `failedChildren > 0`, mapping to a distinct `RunState` in `docs/runtime/run-state.mdx`'s table (e.g. `succeeded-with-failures`). Keep it terminal/non-fatal so existing `continueOnFail` semantics still don't abort the run, but make the masking observable. Gate the new status behind the existing telemetry so callers that only check `finished === success` still work, while new surfaces can distinguish it.
3. **Emit an event.** Persist a `RunStatusChanged`/`RunDegraded` record (the run-state doc already references `RunStatusChanged` at `docs/runtime/run-state.mdx:138`) carrying the failed-child count so the CLI/Gateway/DevTools can render it without re-deriving from per-node rows.

Minimum viable fix is (1) — surfacing `failedChildren` on the result and in `inspect` — even if a new status string is deferred.
